title: RAG Evaluation: Precision, Recall, MRR, nDCG, Faithfulness, Answer Relevance

questions.question: Why is recall@k usually the headline retrieval metric in production RAG?
options: Recall ignores ranking
Generation can drop irrelevant chunks but cannot invent an answer from a chunk it never saw, so missing the right chunk is the more expensive failure
Recall is required by the OpenAPI spec
Recall is the same as precision in RAG
questions.explanation: A retriever that misses the right chunk cannot be repaired downstream; surplus chunks waste tokens but rarely poison the answer.

questions.question: What does MRR compute?
options: The mean of 1 / position-of-first-relevant-document across queries
The geometric mean of cosine similarity
Median rank of the second relevant document
Token count of the answer
questions.explanation: MRR is dominated by the top of the list: rank 1 contributes 1.0, rank 2 contributes 0.5, rank 10 contributes 0.1.

questions.question: When should you reach for nDCG instead of recall@k?
options: When the corpus is small
When the gold labels carry graded relevance (e.g. doc A is 3, doc B is 2, doc C is 1) and binary metrics lose information
When the retriever does not return scores
Never
questions.explanation: nDCG uses graded gains and a position discount; recall flattens everything to binary.

questions.question: What is the difference between faithfulness and answer relevance?
options: Faithfulness asks 'is the answer grounded in the retrieved context'; answer relevance asks 'does the answer address the question'
Faithfulness measures precision; relevance measures recall
Faithfulness is a retrieval metric and relevance is a chunker metric
They are the same metric with two names
questions.explanation: A faithful but off-topic answer scores high on faithfulness, low on relevance; a relevant but ungrounded answer scores high on relevance, low on faithfulness.

questions.question: A pipeline shows decent recall@5 but low MRR. Which stage should you investigate first?
options: The chunker
The reranker, because the right chunk is in the top-k but not at the top
The generator
The corpus ingestor
questions.explanation: Recall@5 means the gold is in top-5; low MRR means it is not at the top of the list. That is the rerank stage's job.

questions.question: What production hazard makes a frozen qrels file dangerous over time?
options: The retriever stops working after six months
Qrels rot: the corpus changes and a doc that was the gold answer last quarter is no longer the right answer; metrics get reported against stale labels
Embedding vectors expire
The eval suite uses up disk space
questions.explanation: Schedule a quarterly qrels review or your metrics will be measuring the wrong thing as the corpus evolves.
